title: Buttonwood Agreement signed
blurb: Twenty-four brokers agree outside 68 Wall Street to trade only with each other at a fixed 0.25% commission, founding what becomes the NYSE.

title: New York Stock & Exchange Board formed
blurb: The Buttonwood brokers adopt a formal constitution and membership rules, institutionalizing organized exchange trading.

title: Dow Jones Industrial Average debuts
blurb: Charles Dow publishes a 12-stock arithmetic average on May 26, creating the first widely tracked US equity benchmark.

title: The Great Crash
blurb: The Dow falls 12.8% on Black Monday (Oct 28) and ~12% on Black Tuesday (Oct 29), beginning a slide that bottoms 89% below peak in 1932.

title: SEC created by the Securities Exchange Act
blurb: Signed June 6, the Act establishes the SEC to regulate secondary trading, following the 1933 Act's disclosure rules for new issues.

title: S&P 500 launched
blurb: Standard & Poor's expands its index to 500 market-cap-weighted stocks computed by computer, creating the modern US equity benchmark.

title: Nasdaq goes live
blurb: On February 8, Nasdaq opens as the first electronic quotation system, displaying competing market-maker prices on screens.

title: May Day deregulation
blurb: On May 1 the SEC abolishes fixed commissions, ending 183 years of cartel pricing and launching the discount-brokerage era.

title: First retail index fund
blurb: Vanguard's John Bogle launches the First Index Investment Trust on August 31, seeding the passive-investing revolution.

title: Black Monday
blurb: On October 19 the Dow drops 22.6% in one session, its worst day ever, amplified by automated portfolio-insurance selling.

title: Dot-com bubble peaks
blurb: The Nasdaq Composite tops at 5,048.62 on March 10 before falling 78% by October 2002.

title: Decimalization
blurb: US exchanges switch to penny price increments from sixteenths, compressing spreads and accelerating electronic and high-frequency trading.

title: Global Financial Crisis
blurb: Lehman Brothers files the largest US bankruptcy on September 15; the S&P 500 ends the year down 38.5%.

title: Flash Crash
blurb: On May 6 the Dow plunges nearly 1,000 points and recovers within ~36 minutes, exposing the fragility of fragmented electronic markets.

title: COVID crash
blurb: The S&P 500 falls ~34% into its March 23 bottom in the fastest 30% drawdown on record, tripping circuit breakers four times.

title: Meme-stock squeeze
blurb: Reddit-driven retail traders drive GameStop from ~$17 to $483 in January, forcing brokerages to halt buying amid collateral strain.
